Q1: I kept things pretty simple and structured because that's what works for me. I'd set aside about two hours each day, usually in the evenings after my other responsibilities, and I'd work through whatever was due next. I'm a visual learner, so I'd print out slides and diagrams when I could afford the printing costs, then study from those. My routine wasn't fancy, but it was consistent enough to keep me on track with assignments.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, though I'll admit deadlines definitely made me push harder during certain weeks. I tried to stick to my evening study blocks, but sometimes life got in the way and I'd have to compress everything closer to due dates. With five online courses running simultaneously, I had to be strategic about which one got attention on which days. The routine helped me stay sane even when things felt overwhelming.
Q3: Honestly, I logged in when I needed to check what was due or submit something. I'd also go in to watch the lecture videos since those were essential for understanding the material visually. Sometimes I'd check the discussion boards to see if anyone had asked questions I was also wondering about. It wasn't something I did casually—each login had a purpose because my time and internet access were limited.
Q4: Yes, eventually checking the LMS became automatic, like part of my morning coffee routine. I'd log in early to see announcements and plan my day around what needed attention. After a few weeks, I just knew that Mondays meant checking for new content and Fridays meant making sure nothing was about to be due. It became muscle memory, even if I wasn't spending hours browsing around.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely the most valuable for me because I learn best when I can see concepts explained visually. I'd watch them carefully, sometimes pausing to sketch diagrams in my notebook. The assignment submission portal was also crucial—knowing exactly what was expected and having clear rubrics helped me focus my limited study time effectively. Anything with visual elements like charts or infographics really stuck with me.
Q6: I rarely engaged deeply with the discussion forums beyond reading them. Posting felt time-consuming, and with my limited resources, I prioritized activities that directly impacted my grades. Some of the supplementary readings I'd skim or skip entirely if they weren't clearly connected to assessments. I know that sounds bad, but when you're juggling five courses with limited time and resources, you have to make tough choices.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams. If I could tell that understanding this video or reading this document would help me complete something graded, I was fully invested. I also valued anything that presented information visually and clearly—dense text walls without diagrams felt like a waste of my precious study hours. Efficiency mattered a lot to me.
Q8: Required activities got my full attention because they affected my grade, plain and simple. Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford with my schedule and the demands of managing five courses. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional content, but I had to be realistic about what I could accomplish. If optional meant truly optional, I treated it that way unless I had rare extra time.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I had good internet access, then study them later without being connected. I also used my own handwritten notes extensively—rewriting things helps me remember them visually. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos or free resources that explained concepts better than the course materials, especially for topics I found confusing.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with printed materials or my handwritten notes at home, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd spend hours reviewing diagrams I'd drawn, testing myself with flashcards I made, or discussing concepts with a study partner in person. All that learning happened completely outside the system. My exam scores reflect that effort even if my login frequency doesn't.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach actually. I'd access a video or document once, download or screenshot the key parts, and then work with those materials offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned early to grab what I needed and study independently. The LMS might show one view, but that material got reviewed multiple times on my end.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially mid-semester when assignments from all five courses seemed to pile up simultaneously. I felt sad and drained during those times, even though I tried to push through. The CBT sessions I attended helped me manage the stress, but some weeks I just felt like I was barely keeping my head above water. My attendance in live sessions suffered because I had to prioritize assignment completion.
Q13: Workload was the biggest factor affecting my LMS use. When I was overwhelmed, I became very strategic—only accessing exactly what I needed for immediate deadlines. Fatigue made it hard to engage with discussion posts or optional content because I simply didn't have the mental energy. Competing responsibilities outside of school also meant I couldn't spend as much time logged in as I might have wanted.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other deadlines or personal issues. I'd realize I'd watched ten minutes without absorbing anything and have to rewind. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't really processing the information. Those moments felt discouraging.
Q15: I sometimes postponed discussion posts because they felt less urgent than assignment submissions. I knew participating was important for learning, but when I had to choose between writing a forum response and finishing a graded assignment, the assignment always won. I also avoided some readings when I was feeling particularly low emotionally—the motivation just wasn't there even though I knew I should engage.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd check the calendar obsessively and work backward from due dates to plan my week. My activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due, then drop off once I submitted. It wasn't the healthiest approach, but it's how I managed to maintain my high assignment completion rate across all five courses.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced me when I received it. Positive feedback motivated me to maintain my approach, while constructive criticism helped me adjust for the next assignment. However, sometimes feedback came too late to really change my habits for that particular course. I appreciated detailed comments more than just grades because they helped me understand what to improve visually in my work.
Q18: The course structure was pretty clear, which I appreciated. Having a predictable rhythm of content release and due dates helped me plan my limited study time effectively. The modular organization meant I could tackle things in chunks rather than feeling overwhelmed by everything at once. That structure was essential for someone like me managing multiple courses simultaneously.
Q19: Clear visual organization of the LMS made engagement easier—when I could see at a glance what was due and what resources were available, I felt more in control. What made it harder was when important information was buried in long text announcements or when resources weren't clearly labeled. Also, required live sessions were difficult with my schedule and attendance suffered because of that.
Q20: I think students might be searching for understanding, clicking through materials trying to make sense of things, but struggling to produce quality work. Or maybe they're dealing with personal issues that make it hard to focus enough to complete assignments even though they're trying. High activity could also mean someone is disorganized and keeps returning to find things they missed. It doesn't always mean productive engagement.
Q21: This actually describes me somewhat. My LMS activity looks low, but I performed well because most of my real learning happened offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. I'm efficient when I log in—I get what I need and leave. My exam scores and assignment completion show that I understood the material even if I wasn't constantly clicking around the platform.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show submission patterns, deadline compliance, and which resources students accessed. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of attention during access, and the emotional state of the learner. They can't capture whether someone truly understood something or just clicked through it. The numbers tell a very incomplete story about actual learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and apply the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, making connections to other things I know, and caring about whether I truly get it. It's mental investment, not just time spent logged in. Some of my most engaged moments happened with a pencil and paper, nowhere near a computer.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on visual learning strategies like drawing diagrams and creating my own charts. It wouldn't capture the emotional effort it took to stay motivated while feeling sad and stressed, or how the CBT sessions helped me keep going. It also misses that I was genuinely invested in learning despite my low login frequency—I just learned differently than the system was designed to track.
